sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Nausea and vomiting are common symptoms with many possible causes, ranging from self-limited gastroenteritis to bowel obstruction, pregnancy-related illness, toxic exposure, or serious intracranial and cardiac disease.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Hypersalivation, retching, clamminess, sweating, pallor, or abdominal discomfort
- Associated headache, dizziness, fever, diarrhoea, or abdominal pain
- Red flags include intractable vomiting, haematemesis, altered conscious state, bilious or faeculent vomit, and significant dehydration

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Ondansetron can prolong the QT interval; use caution in known or suspected long-QT syndrome or other significant QT-risk settings
- Use of ondansetron in early pregnancy should follow local policy and clinician judgement; dehydration and inability to tolerate fluids are higher prehospital priorities
- Pregnant patients with uncontrolled hyperemesis are not suitable to remain on site
- Nausea and vomiting may be medication related, including GLP-1 agonists such as semaglutide or tirzepatide
- If the cause cannot be determined, consider serious underlying disease such as bowel obstruction, intracranial pathology, sepsis, toxic exposure, or ACS

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey
- Complete vital signs, including blood glucose where appropriate, and assess hydration, abdominal findings, pregnancy risk, and red flags
- Position to reduce aspiration risk and provide emesis receptacle support as needed
- Consider a likely cause and provide non-invasive symptom relief or antiemetic therapy within scope
- Encourage oral rehydration only if the patient can safely tolerate small amounts of fluid
- Consider urinalysis where dehydration, pregnancy, or urinary pathology is relevant
- Escalate early if persistent vomiting, haematemesis, altered conscious state, severe pain, or significant dehydration is present

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Consider IV access if parenteral medication, fluids, or broader resuscitation may be required
- Consider IV fluids where clinically indicated, guided by dehydration severity, comorbidity, and ongoing losses
- Consider ECG if there is QT-risk, significant electrolyte concern, or possible cardiac mimic
- Consider point-of-care blood testing where available and clinically useful
- Treat the likely underlying cause in line with the relevant condition-specific guideline and transport if red flags, refractory symptoms, or diagnostic uncertainty persist

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Ondansetron is less effective for vestibular causes of nausea and vomiting
- Isopropyl alcohol swab inhalation may provide short-term nausea relief in some patients
- Undifferentiated nausea and vomiting may reflect ACS, opioid effects, intracranial disease, severe gastroenteritis, or bowel obstruction
